nodes.label: Attention Is All You Need
nodes.title: Attention Is All You Need
nodes.abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. On two machine translation tasks, these models are superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
nodes.s2_tldr: A new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms.

nodes.label: attention mechanism
nodes.description: A fundamental component of transformer models that selects relevant tokens for processing.

nodes.label: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
nodes.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
nodes.abstract: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.
nodes.s2_tldr: BERT pre-trains deep bidirectional representations by conditioning on both left and right context.

nodes.label: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
nodes.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
nodes.abstract: We demonstrate that scaling language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, and test its performance in the few-shot setting.
nodes.s2_tldr: GPT-3 demonstrates that scaling language models greatly improves few-shot task performance.

nodes.label: Efficient Attention via Control Variates
nodes.title: Efficient Attention via Control Variates
nodes.abstract: Random-feature-based attention (RFA) is an efficient approximation of softmax attention with linear runtime and space complexity. However, the approximation gap between RFA and conventional softmax attention is not well studied. Built upon previous progress of RFA, we characterize this gap through the lens of control variates and show that RFA can be decomposed into a sum of multiple control variate estimators for each element in the sequence. This new framework reveals that exact softmax attention can be recovered from RFA by manipulating each control variate. Besides, it allows us to develop a more flexible form of control variates, resulting in a novel attention mechanism that significantly reduces the approximation gap while maintaining linear complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms state-of-the-art efficient attention mechanisms on both vision and language tasks.
nodes.s2_tldr: This new framework reveals that exact softmax attention can be recovered from RFA by manipulating each control variate, resulting in a novel attention mechanism that significantly reduces the approximation gap while maintaining linear complexity.

nodes.label: Random-Feature Attention
nodes.description: Efficient approximation of softmax attention with linear runtime and space complexity using random features

nodes.label: Softmax Attention
nodes.description: Conventional attention mechanism that RFA approximates, serving as the baseline for comparison

nodes.label: Control Variates
nodes.description: Statistical technique used to characterize and reduce the approximation gap between RFA and softmax attention

nodes.label: Approximation Gap
nodes.description: The difference in performance between random-feature-based attention and exact softmax attention

nodes.label: Linear Complexity Attention
nodes.description: Attention mechanisms maintaining linear runtime and space complexity

nodes.label: Benign Overfitting in Token Selection of Attention Mechanism
nodes.title: Benign Overfitting in Token Selection of Attention Mechanism
nodes.abstract: Attention mechanism is a fundamental component of the transformer model and plays a significant role in its success. However, the theoretical understanding of how attention learns to select tokens is still an emerging area of research. In this work, we study the training dynamics and generalization ability of the attention mechanism under classification problems with label noise. We show that, with the characterization of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), the token selection of attention mechanism achieves benign overfitting, i.e., maintaining high generalization performance despite fitting label noise. Our work also demonstrates an interesting delayed acquisition of generalization after an initial phase of overfitting. Finally, we provide experiments to support our theoretical analysis using both synthetic and real-world datasets.
nodes.s2_tldr: It is shown that, with the characterization of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), the token selection of attention mechanism achieves benign overfitting, i.e., maintaining high generalization performance despite fitting label noise.

nodes.label: Benign Overfitting
nodes.description: The phenomenon where a model fits label noise yet maintains high generalization performance.

nodes.label: Signal-to-Noise Ratio
nodes.description: A characterization used to analyze token selection behavior in attention mechanisms.

nodes.label: Token Selection
nodes.description: The process by which the attention mechanism identifies and weights relevant input tokens.

nodes.label: Label Noise
nodes.description: Incorrect or noisy labels present in training data used in classification problems.

nodes.label: Delayed Generalization
nodes.description: A phenomenon where generalization ability is acquired after an initial phase of overfitting.

nodes.label: Training Dynamics
nodes.description: The behavior and evolution of model parameters during the training process.

nodes.label: Self-Attention as Distributional Projection: A Unified Interpretation of Transformer Architecture
nodes.title: Self-Attention as Distributional Projection: A Unified Interpretation of Transformer Architecture
nodes.abstract: This paper presents a mathematical interpretation of self-attention by connecting it to distributional semantics principles. We show that self-attention emerges from projecting corpus-level co-occurrence statistics into sequence context. Starting from the co-occurrence matrix underlying GloVe embeddings, we demonstrate how the projection naturally captures contextual influence, with the query-key-value mechanism arising as the natural asymmetric extension for modeling directional relationships. Positional encodings and multi-head attention then follow as structured refinements of this same projection principle. Our analysis demonstrates that the Transformer architecture's particular algebraic form follows from these projection principles rather than being an arbitrary design choice.
nodes.s2_tldr: This paper presents a mathematical interpretation of self-attention by connecting it to distributional semantics principles, and demonstrates that the Transformer architecture's particular algebraic form follows from these projection principles rather than being an arbitrary design choice.

nodes.label: self-attention mechanism
nodes.description: Mathematical operation in Transformers interpreted as projecting corpus-level co-occurrence statistics into sequence context

nodes.label: distributional semantics
nodes.description: Principles used to provide mathematical interpretation of self-attention

nodes.label: co-occurrence matrix
nodes.description: Corpus-level statistical structure underlying GloVe embeddings, used as foundation for the projection framework

nodes.label: query-key-value mechanism
nodes.description: Asymmetric extension of projection principle for modeling directional relationships

nodes.label: positional encodings
nodes.description: Structured refinements of the projection principle within Transformer architecture

nodes.label: multi-head attention
nodes.description: Structured refinement of projection principle in the Transformer architecture

nodes.label: Transformer architecture
nodes.description: Architecture whose algebraic form is shown to follow from projection principles

nodes.label: Random Feature Attention
nodes.description: An efficient approximation of softmax attention with linear runtime and space complexity using random features.

nodes.label: Self-Attention
nodes.description: Mechanism interpreted as projection of corpus-level co-occurrence statistics into sequence context

nodes.label: Co-occurrence Statistics
nodes.description: Corpus-level statistics underlying GloVe embeddings used as basis for deriving self-attention

nodes.label: Linear Complexity
nodes.description: Linear runtime and space complexity achieved by efficient attention mechanisms.

nodes.label: Skilling and deskilling: technological change in classical economic theory and its empirical evidence
nodes.title: Skilling and deskilling: technological change in classical economic theory and its empirical evidence
nodes.abstract: This article reviews and brings together two literatures: classical political economists’ views on the skilling or deskilling nature of technological change in England, during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries when they wrote, are compared with the empirical evidence about the skill effects of technological change that emerges from studies of economic historians. In both literatures, we look at both the skill impacts of technological change and at the “inducement mechanisms” that are envisaged for the introduction of new technologies. Adam Smith and Karl Marx both regarded the deskilling of the labour force as the predominant form of biased technical change, but other authors such as Charles Babbage also took account of capital-skill complementarities and skill-enhancing effects of technological change. For Smith, the deskilling bias was an unintended by-product of the increasing division of labour, which in his view “naturally” led to ever more simplification of workers’ tasks. As opposed to Smith, Marx considered unskilled-biased technical change as a bourgeois weapon in the class struggle for impairing the workers’ bargaining position. Studies of economic historians lend support to Marx’s hypothesis about the inducement mechanisms for the introduction of unskilled-biased innovations, but have produced no clear-cut empirical evidence for a deskilling tendency of eighteenth- and nineteenth-century technological change as a whole. Industrialization in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries rather led to labour polarization, by simultaneously deskilling a large part of the workforce and raising the demand for some (but fewer) high-skilled workers.

nodes.label: Deskilling
nodes.description: The process by which technological change reduces the skill requirements of workers' tasks

nodes.label: Capital-skill complementarity
nodes.description: The phenomenon where capital investment and skilled labour complement each other, raising demand for skilled workers

nodes.label: Biased technical change
nodes.description: Technological change that favours certain types of labour (skilled or unskilled) over others

nodes.label: Labour polarization
nodes.description: Simultaneous deskilling of a large part of the workforce while raising demand for some high-skilled workers

nodes.label: Division of labour
nodes.description: Increasing specialization of workers' tasks, seen by Smith as naturally leading to simplification and deskilling

nodes.label: Inducement mechanisms
nodes.description: The reasons or forces that drive the introduction of new technologies with particular skill biases

nodes.label: Unskilled-biased innovation
nodes.description: Technological innovations that reduce the relative demand for skilled labour

nodes.label: Understanding the Success of the Know-Nothing Party
nodes.title: Understanding the Success of the Know-Nothing Party
nodes.abstract: We study the contribution of economic conditions to the success of the first avowedly nativist political party in the United States.The Know-Nothing Party gained control of a number of state governments in the 1854-1856 elections running on a staunchly anti-Catholic and anti-Irish platform.Our analysis focuses on the case of Massachusetts, which had experienced a wave of Irish Famine immigration and was at the forefront of industrialization in the United States.Voters in towns with more exposure to Irish labor market crowdout and deskilling in manufacturing were more likely to vote for Know-Nothing candidates in state elections.These two forces played a decisive role in 1855, but not the other years of the Know Nothings' success.We find evidence of reduced wealth accumulation for native workers most exposed to labor market crowdout and deskilling, though this was tempered by occupational upgrading.

nodes.label: Nativist Political Party
nodes.description: Political party running on anti-immigrant platforms, specifically anti-Catholic and anti-Irish in the US mid-19th century

nodes.label: Labor Market Crowdout
nodes.description: Displacement of native workers from jobs due to influx of immigrant labor

nodes.label: Manufacturing Deskilling
nodes.description: Reduction in skill requirements for manufacturing jobs due to industrialization or labor substitution

nodes.label: Irish Famine Immigration
nodes.description: Wave of Irish immigrants to the US driven by the Great Famine in Ireland

nodes.label: Occupational Upgrading
nodes.description: Native workers moving to higher-skilled or better-paid occupations in response to immigrant competition

nodes.label: Wealth Accumulation
nodes.description: Accumulation of wealth by native workers, found to be reduced by labor market exposure to immigrants

nodes.label: Technology, Vintage-Specific Human Capital, and Labor Displacement: Evidence from Linking Patents with Occupations
nodes.title: Technology, Vintage-Specific Human Capital, and Labor Displacement: Evidence from Linking Patents with Occupations
nodes.abstract: We develop a measure of workers' technology exposure that relies only on textual descriptions of patent documents and the tasks performed by workers in an occupation.Our measure appears to identify a combination of labor-saving innovations but also technologies that may require skills that incumbent workers lack.Using a panel of administrative data, we examine how subsequent worker earnings relate to workers' technology exposure.We find that workers at both the bottom but also the top of the earnings distribution are displaced.Our interpretation is that low-paid workers are displaced as their tasks are automated while the highest-paid workers face lower earnings growth as some of their skills become obsolete.Our calibrated model fits these facts and emphasizes the importance of movements in skill quantities, not just skill prices, for the link between technology and inequality.

nodes.label: Technology exposure measure
nodes.description: A measure based on textual descriptions of patent documents and occupational task descriptions to quantify workers' exposure to new technologies

nodes.label: Labor-saving innovations
nodes.description: Technologies that reduce the need for human labor in performing certain tasks

nodes.label: Skill obsolescence
nodes.description: Process by which incumbent workers' existing skills become less valuable due to new technologies

nodes.label: Task automation
nodes.description: Replacement of worker tasks by automated technologies, particularly affecting low-paid workers

nodes.label: Earnings displacement
nodes.description: Reduction in worker earnings resulting from technology exposure, affecting both bottom and top earners

nodes.label: Skill quantities vs prices
nodes.description: Distinction between the amounts of skills in the labor market and their market prices as drivers of inequality
